title: Greek Roasted Vegetables

subs.name: Ridge gourd (turai) or bottlegourd (lauki) are reasonable Indian substitutes that absorb the olive oil and herb flavours well, though the texture is slightly different
Any variety of baingan (eggplant/brinjal) is identical to what is used in Greece; long purple or round desi varieties both work well

ingredients.name: potatoes, peeled and cut into 1 cm rounds

subs.name: The olive oil is the dressing and flavour vehicle here — reduce the quantity to 3 tablespoons and supplement with a tablespoon of butter if using a neutral oil
Dried thyme or mixed Italian herbs give a similar Mediterranean flavour; fresh basil added after cooking (not during) gives a different but lovely result

steps.text: Preheat the oven to 180°C. In a large bowl, toss the zucchini, brinjal, and potato with the olive oil, garlic, oregano, and a generous amount of salt until everything is well coated. Spread the vegetable mix into a large, deep baking dish or roasting tin.

Lay the sliced tomatoes over the top of the vegetable mix, overlapping them slightly. Scatter the onion slices over the tomatoes. Pour the water or stock around the edges of the dish — this creates enough steam to braise the bottom layer of vegetables even as the top roasts.

Roast at 180°C for 50–60 minutes, checking once at the 30-minute mark. If the top is colouring too fast, loosely cover with foil. The briam is ready when the potatoes are completely tender, the zucchini and brinjal are soft, and the tomatoes on top are collapsed and beginning to caramelise at the edges.

Remove from the oven and allow to settle for 10 minutes — the vegetables continue to absorb the herb-infused olive oil as they rest. Scatter fresh parsley over the top before serving. Serve warm or at room temperature with bread for mopping up the oily juices in the dish.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: zucchini → Ridge gourd (turai) or bottlegourd (lauki) are reasonable Indian substitutes that absorb the olive oil and herb flavours well, though the texture is slightly different; brinjal → Any variety of baingan (eggplant/brinjal) is identical to what is used in Greece; long purple or round desi varieties both work well; olive-oil → The olive oil is the dressing and flavour vehicle here — reduce the quantity to 3 tablespoons and supplement with a tablespoon of butter if using a neutral oil; oregano → Dried thyme or mixed Italian herbs give a similar Mediterranean flavour; fresh basil added after cooking (not during) gives a different but lovely result
indian_kitchen_adaptation: An OTG or any countertop oven handles briam perfectly. For a stovetop version, layer all the vegetables in a deep, heavy kadai with a tight-fitting lid over very low heat, adding the water or stock and olive oil, and cook for 45–50 minutes until the vegetables are completely soft — you will not get the caramelised edges of the oven version, but the flavour is largely the same. All vegetables (zucchini, brinjal, potato, tomato, onion) are completely standard in India. Dried oregano is available at large grocery chains. The dish is best made with a good quality olive oil since it is also the sauce.
